explanation.text: The narrator and Yolanda spent hours programming and adjusting SwiftBot until he could obey “play offense,” catch the ball, and dribble with control. These details reveal actions the robot has been taught.

From the triple-threat stance SwiftBot can either dribble or shoot, and the narrator eagerly waits to learn which option he will choose. The sentence therefore shows uncertainty about the robot's next action.
SwiftBot jumps beyond the narrator's reach and calculates a perfect, rimless shot. Watching this achievement with “eyes wide” shows that the narrator is impressed by the robot's ability.

Backspin is a subtle human movement that SwiftBot's mechanical fingers and tactile sensors cannot yet copy. The narrator's repeated wish to engineer it shows his desire to add more human-like abilities.
After releasing the layup, the narrator follows the ball with his eyes to see whether it reaches the target and basket. In this context, “tracked” therefore suggests close visual attention.

SwiftBot repeatedly fell while learning to jump and dented his casing badly enough that the narrator had to hammer it back into shape. That repeated repair is concrete evidence of the difficulty involved in teaching the sport.

After the game, the human narrator is exhausted, breathing hard, and urgently drinking water, while SwiftBot neither tires nor becomes dehydrated. This direct contrast shows a machine's physical advantage.

The section explains that corvids must identify food, patiently solve access problems, and adapt their strategies to wolves, cities, and trash bins. These examples support the central idea that special intelligence helps them survive.
The phrase appears beside learning, creativity, and adapting when familiar food or nesting conditions disappear. Therefore, “behavioral flexibility” means changing behavior to solve new problems.
A blue whale has a much heavier brain, but it is less than 0.01 percent of its huge body; a corvid's brain is about 2.4 percent of its body. Thus, the corvid has the greater brain-to-body ratio.

Paragraph 2 claims corvids creatively solve complex problems. The raven's need to think, wait, and safely steal food from feeding wolves therefore provides a specific example of that problem-solving intelligence.

A scavenging corvid must first recognize needed food and then invent a safe way to reach it without confronting predators. That detail directly demonstrates intelligence producing a solution when necessity arises.

The immigrants face a sheer green mountain wall, heavy humidity, and a slow climb with all their burdens. Saying the landscape “dar[es] us to scale it” therefore presents the new country as a formidable challenge.

Although Ichiro cannot understand the Brazilian boy's language, their shared chase creates connection, and he becomes certain he will soon understand everything. That confidence shows hope that life in Brazil will change positively.

The boy's relief, gratitude, and humor after Ichiro catches the piglet create a friendly exchange despite their language difference. That successful connection therefore makes Ichiro hopeful that he can adjust to Brazil.
Ichiro “stumbled” while carrying a bundle, the children “clutched” one another, and Father “lumbered” under heavy bags. These verbs therefore emphasize the family's difficult movement off the ship.

Because the journey brings humidity, rot, nausea, and struggle while old Ichiro most vividly recalls the joy of helping the boy and every feature of his face, his lasting positive reaction outweighs the discomforts.

Ichiro concentrates on the heat, rotten odor, nausea, and other immediate hardships. His father instead smiles and says Brazil is “beyond my imagination,” therefore indicating that the country exceeds what he expected.

Decades later, Ichiro still recalls the Brazilian boy's face in rich, affectionate detail, while the earlier heat, sickness, and fear no longer dominate his memory. The ending therefore shows a positive milestone outlasting its hardships.
The cliff overhang sheltered the dwelling from weather, allowing it to remain complete hundreds of years after its abandonment. In this context, “intact” means perfectly preserved.

The opening describes the cowboys' 1888 discovery of an ancient settlement. Paragraph 7 then explains that thousands of Ancestral Puebloans had built a thriving community there for seven centuries, supplying its historical context.
The Ancestral Puebloans are master builders, skilled craftspeople, successful dryland farmers, and traders. Calling them “adept” therefore emphasizes their broad set of survival skills.

The article moves from simple pit houses to multistory adobe homes and finally to sophisticated masonry dwellings built into cliffs. This progression shows the Ancestral Puebloans' architectural advancement.

Calling Mesa Verde a “thriving community” therefore conveys the author's positive interpretation of its long, populous history. The other statements mainly report construction details or locations without that evaluative language.

The section traces threats from looters to the creation of a national park whose purpose is government protection and preservation of Mesa Verde. That protective mission is therefore the section's central idea.

The author follows the cowboys as they track cattle, look across the canyon, circle to the other side, and descend toward the dwellings. Recounting this journey therefore introduces both the Mesa Verde landscape and the discovery.
